meta.name: PCL-5 PTSD Checklist
meta.description: The PCL-5 is a 20-item self-report measure assessing DSM-5 PTSD symptom severity across four clusters: intrusion, avoidance, negative alterations in cognition and mood, and alterations in arousal and reactivity.
meta.author: Weathers et al. (2013)
meta.reference: Weathers FW, Litz BT, Keane TM, Palmieri PA, Marx BP, Schnurr PP. The PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5). National Center for PTSD; 2013.
meta.instruction: Below is a list of problems that people sometimes have in response to a very stressful experience. Please read each problem carefully and then indicate how much you have been bothered by that problem in the past month.

questions.text: Repeated, disturbing, and unwanted memories of the stressful experience
Repeated, disturbing dreams of the stressful experience
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again (as if you were actually back there reliving it)
Feeling very upset when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience (for example, heart pounding, trouble breathing, sweating)
Avoiding memories, thoughts, or feelings related to the stressful experience
Avoiding external reminders of the stressful experience (for example, people, places, conversations, activities, objects, or situations)
Trouble remembering important parts of the stressful experience
Having strong negative beliefs about yourself, other people, or the world
Blaming yourself or someone else for the stressful experience or what happened after it
Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame
Loss of interest in activities that you used to enjoy
Feeling distant or cut off from other people
Trouble experiencing positive feelings (for example, being unable to feel happiness or have loving feelings for people close to you)
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively
Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
Being superalert or watchful or on guard
Feeling jumpy or easily startled
Having difficulty concentrating
Trouble falling or staying asleep

ranges.label: Below PTSD Threshold
ranges.description: Your PTSD symptom score is low, below the clinical diagnostic threshold. This indicates that you are not currently showing significant post-traumatic stress reactions, and your psychological recovery is progressing well.
suggestions: If you still feel distressed, professional help is available
Continue healthy coping strategies
Monitor your emotional state
Continue maintaining your good psychological health
Stay emotionally connected with friends and family

ranges.label: Mild PTSD Symptoms
ranges.description: You have some PTSD symptoms near or at clinical significance. Mild trauma-related reactions are normal after experiencing stressful events and typically diminish over time.
suggestions: Consider consulting a mental health professional
Practice relaxation and grounding techniques
Maintain your social support network
Allow yourself time and space to process your emotions
Share your feelings with someone you trust

ranges.label: Moderate PTSD Symptoms
ranges.description: Your PTSD symptoms are moderate. The suggested clinical cutoff for PCL-5 is 33. At this level, trauma responses may include flashbacks, avoidance behaviors, or emotional numbness that affect daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Strongly recommended to seek a trauma-specialized therapist
Consider evidence-based trauma treatments (CPT, PE, or EMDR)
Maintain regular daily routines
Avoid using alcohol or substances to cope
Consider consulting a professional trained in trauma therapy

ranges.label: Severe PTSD Symptoms
ranges.description: Your PTSD symptoms are severe and likely significantly impact daily functioning. Your trauma-related reactions are quite pronounced and may be significantly impacting your functioning. Professional trauma-focused support is recommended.
suggestions: Please seek professional trauma treatment immediately
If in crisis, contact 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline
Create a safety plan for managing intense distress
Follow professional treatment recommendations closely
Contact a trauma therapy specialist as soon as possible

intrusion.label: Low
intrusion.description: Intrusion symptoms are low. This indicates that you are not currently showing significant post-traumatic stress reactions, and your psychological recovery is progressing well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your good psychological health
Stay emotionally connected with friends and family
Focus on personal growth and positive experiences
Remember to seek support if you face difficult events in the future

intrusion.label: Mild
intrusion.description: Some intrusion symptoms present. Mild trauma-related reactions are normal after experiencing stressful events and typically diminish over time.
suggestions: Allow yourself time and space to process your emotions
Share your feelings with someone you trust
Maintain routines and structure in your daily life
Practice grounding techniques (such as focusing on your five senses) to stay present
Avoid repeated exposure to trauma-related information or content

intrusion.label: Moderate
intrusion.description: Notable intrusion symptoms. At this level, trauma responses may include flashbacks, avoidance behaviors, or emotional numbness that affect daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consider consulting a professional trained in trauma therapy
Trauma-Focused CBT (TF-CBT) or EMDR may be helpful
Build a sense of safety and daily routine
Learn emotional regulation and coping skills
Avoid using alcohol or substances to cope

intrusion.label: Severe
intrusion.description: Severe intrusion symptoms. Your trauma-related reactions are quite pronounced and may be significantly impacting your functioning. Professional trauma-focused support is recommended.
suggestions: Contact a trauma therapy specialist as soon as possible
Process traumatic memories in a safe environment
Develop a safety plan and crisis coping strategies
Ensure you have a supportive social network
Follow professional guidance for a gradual recovery process
